figures.caption: Figure 1: Sketch of the two-body preconditioner idea. Large blue dots indicate coarse surface discretization (MFS collocation nodes; source points are not shown). Small red dots indicate a more expensive fine discretization. (a) In dilute suspensions, a coarse discretization of each particle suffices to resolve all interactions, and a (one-body) basis for the flow field is obtained by using MFS to solve each body in isolation (inset). (b) In denser systems, any particle undergoing a near contact demands a fine discretization to capture local hydrodynamics. (c) The proposed scheme constructs a basis in which such fine discretizations are used only at the level of isolated pairs (inset), with each such interaction compressed to an equivalent coarse representation in the global solve. Fine grids are retained only locally for resolving near-field interactions and post-processing. In each panel and each inset, light blue indicates the fluid domain for the BVP solved.

Figure 2: Mobility solution for a random close packing of 10,000 unit circles (packing density ϕ = 0.65, minimum separation 10−3), with each particle driven by a unit torque. The global iterative solve uses 72 vector unknowns per body and converges in 47 GMRES iterations and 36 s on a single compute node. From left to right, a third of the particles is colored by velocity magnitude, followed by maximum relative boundary residual, and relative rigid body motion error, as defined in (62),(61) and (63). The velocity field shows slower motion in the interior of the cluster. The boundary residual is measured with respect to the boundary data, and the velocity error is computed relative to a finer reference solution.

Figure 3: For the five particles shown in panel (a), the construction of their two-body bases ψ(1), . . . ,ψ(5) is diagrammed in panel (c). For bodies in near contact, the one-body basis is augmented with pairwise corrections η(i,j), each computed by solving a BVP with a single neighbor as in panel (b), using a fine discretization (red). For well-separated particles, no correction is needed: the one-body basis alone suffices, and the coarse discretization (blue) resolves all interactions.

Table 1: Functions appearing in the flow field representations. The same notation is used to describe functions in both the resistance and mobility problems, although their construction is based on different boundary value problems. Here, C(i) denotes the set of indices of bodies in near contact with body i.
Figure 4: Illustration of the linear maps used to construct the correction η(1,2) to the basis of particle 1 (here called the active particle) due to near-contacting particle 2, as in Section 3.2.

Table 2: The four different point sets defined on each particle. We also denote {X(i)}Pi=1 as the full set of coarse source points for all P particles, and similarly for the coarse collocation points. The number of fine source and collocation points used to set up a pair-correction will depend on the particle distance within each pair, see Section 5.
Table 3: Coefficient vectors in solution procedures.

Figure 5: The peanut (magenta) forms a separation or proxy surface; no other disk can get closer to the (1-2) contacting pair. In Section 3.3 this is used, along with matrices C(1-2) and D(1-2), to fill A(1-2) (black arrows), the coarse-to-coarse matrix that compresses the true near-contact pairwise lubrication interaction.
Figure 6: The proxy source points used for the local fine BVPs are enhanced with sources on ellipse segments that shield singularities located up to a distance Racc(δ) from each particle’s center.

Figure 7: Comparison of proposed peanut-compressed two-body preconditioning method (red circles), against one-body preconditioning (blue + signs), for resistance and mobility BVPs. The geometries are random clusters of unit disks with each disk having separation δ from at least one neighbor. Curves show the mean (and shaded color the range) over five randomized runs at each δ.

Figure 8: The mobility problem is solved for a random packing of P particles at packing density ϕ. In panels (a) and (b), P = 500 is fixed while ϕ is varied; in panels (c) and (d), ϕ = 0.65 is fixed while P is varied.
